planting.note: Anchor stem cuttings in moist aquatic substrate or simply drop bunches into still, warm water in full sun in zone 6–11 once frosts have passed; Parrot Feather is a vigorous, feathery South American aquatic plant with emergent, bright-green, whorled, finely divided leaves that extend above the water surface — widely used in water gardens, fish ponds, and aquariums for its ornamental value and oxygenating properties, but invasive outside its native range.

directions: Anchor Myriophyllum aquaticum stem bunches in aquatic substrate at the pond margin or in water 3–12 inches deep in full sun; plants spread vigorously by stem fragments — contain in aquatic pots to prevent spread, and never dispose of cuttings in natural waterways. Parrot Feather is native to the Amazon River basin and adjacent South America and has become naturalized as an invasive aquatic plant throughout much of North America, Europe, Australia, and beyond — spreading through watercourses via stem fragments and posing serious ecological risk. Despite its invasive status, it remains widely sold and planted for its exceptional ornamental value: the bright-blue-green, pine-like emergent shoots are genuinely beautiful and create an attractive, feathery texture at the water's edge. In garden ponds, grow only in contained aquatic pots and never plant near natural waterways, drainage ditches, or irrigation systems. Note: regulated or banned in many US states — check local regulations before purchasing or planting. In zone 6–8, tops die back to the water after hard frost but re-emerge in spring; in zone 9–11, it grows year-round.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Center for Aquatic and Invasive Plants

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained to moist, sandy or loamy soil in zone 9–12; Beach Spider Lily is a tropical bulbous perennial native to coastal sandy habitats from the Caribbean through Central America and tropical South America — bearing spectacular, large, bright-white, fragrant flowers with long, spidery, reflexed tepals and a prominent central cup, and providing remarkable visual impact in coastal and tropical gardens.

directions: Plant Hymenocallis littoralis bulbs at a depth of about 4 inches, with the neck just at or slightly below soil level, in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in a site with morning sun and afternoon shade, or bright indirect light; water moderately and allow soil to dry slightly between waterings; avoid waterlogged conditions which cause bulb rot. Beach Spider Lily differs from the more commonly grown Hymenocallis latifolia (Coastal Spider Lily) in being a strictly tropical species adapted to seasonally dry coastal habitats throughout the Caribbean and tropical Americas — performing best in zone 10–12 and requiring frost protection in zone 9. The flowers are among the most elegant of all bulbous plants: large, pure white, intensely fragrant (especially at night), with six long, recurving, ribbon-like tepals and a central funnel-shaped corona — strikingly beautiful in the evening garden. Plants produce attractive, broad, strap-like, glossy dark-green leaves year-round in frost-free climates; clumps increase slowly by offsets. In zone 9, plant in a sheltered, south-facing location and mulch heavily in winter; in zone 10–12, grow in the open garden with minimal care beyond occasional irrigation during dry periods.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hymenocallis

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring 2–3 inches deep in full sun in well-drained, gritty to loamy soil in zone 7–10; Argentine Rain Lily is a summer-blooming South American bulb from the grasslands of Argentina, Uruguay, and southern Brazil — producing beautiful, large (for the genus), nodding, funnel-shaped, rose-pink flowers on 12-inch stems within days of a soaking summer rain, with each flower held at a characteristic 45-degree angle that distinguishes Habranthus from Zephyranthes.

directions: Plant Habranthus robustus bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; bulbs perform best with a dry dormant period in late spring followed by a good soaking (simulating monsoon rains) to trigger flowering; clumps multiply over time and bloom most profusely when slightly crowded. Argentine Rain Lily is the largest-flowered and most robust species in the Habranthus genus — a group of summer-rain-triggered bulbs closely related to Zephyranthes but distinguished by the characteristic nodding, angled flower posture (held at roughly 45 degrees) rather than upright. Flowers emerge in impressive flushes within 24–72 hours of a summer downpour, creating a breathtaking display: 3–4 inch, rose-pink to pale-pink funnel-shaped flowers with a golden-yellow center, held on slender stems above narrow, rush-like leaves. Exceptionally easy to grow and reliably perennial throughout zone 7–10, including the Gulf Coast where summer rains naturally trigger perfect bloom cycles. Bulbs naturalize readily in lawn edges, rock gardens, and raised beds; they naturalize particularly well in Texas and the Gulf South where the summer rain/dry pattern matches their native South American prairie climate. Divide crowded clumps every 3–4 years and replant offsets to spread.
sources: Scott Ogden, 'Garden Bulbs for the South' (Timber Press)
LSU AgCenter — rain lilies for Louisiana gardens

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring 2–3 inches deep in full sun in well-drained, rocky to sandy-loam soil in zone 7–10; Prairie Rain Lily is a Texas and northeastern Mexico native bulbous perennial — appearing in masses across limestone grasslands, cedar-oak savannas, and roadside prairies after summer rains — with large, pure-white, powerfully fragrant flowers that open at dusk and stay open through the night, making it an outstanding native plant for moon gardens and evening landscapes.

directions: Plant Cooperia pedunculata bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, lean, rocky to sandy-loam soil in full sun; provide a dry period through spring, then allow summer rains (or supplemental soaking) to trigger blooming; avoid overwatering during dormancy as bulbs rot easily in wet winter soils. Prairie Rain Lily is a beloved Texas wildflower that carpets limestone roadsides, ranch grasslands, and cedar-oak savannas with brilliant white after summer rains — sometimes appearing by the thousands seemingly overnight. The genus Cooperia is now often merged into Zephyranthes by modern taxonomists, but the species is distinct in its nocturnal blooming behavior: flowers open fully at dusk, are powerfully sweet-fragrant, pure white, and 2–3 inches wide — perfectly adapted to pollination by sphinx moths and hawk moths that fly at night. A superb choice for naturalistic meadow plantings, moon gardens, and dry-summer landscapes throughout the Texas Hill Country and Gulf Coast. Bulbs naturalize in thin, rocky soil far better than in rich garden beds — excess fertility delays flowering. Divide crowded clumps in late summer after foliage yellows; bulbs can be moved dry during dormancy.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Cooperia pedunculata
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Texas native bulbs
Ogden, 'Garden Bulbs for the South' (Timber Press)

subcategory: Gulf Coast native deciduous conifer swamp tree

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root transplants in early spring in full sun in moist to wet, acid, well-drained to seasonally flooded soil in zone 4–11; Bald Cypress is the quintessential tree of the Gulf Coast and Deep South — native from Delaware to Texas in lowland swamps, river floodplains, and bayou margins — producing graceful, feathery, deciduous foliage that turns brilliant orange-russet in fall, and distinctive 'cypress knees' (pneumatophores) when growing in standing water.

directions: Plant Taxodium distichum in full sun in moist to wet, acidic soil — it tolerates standing water, drought once established, alkaline soils, and urban conditions, making it one of the most adaptable large trees in its range; young trees benefit from regular watering for the first 1–2 years, after which they become remarkably tough and self-sufficient. Bald Cypress is among the most iconic trees of the American South — the defining image of Gulf Coast bayous, cypress swamps, and river floodplains — yet it is equally at home in ordinary garden soil and among the most pest- and disease-resistant of all large deciduous trees. Growing 50–70 feet tall with a buttressed base, strongly vertical form, and spectacular fine-textured, feathery foliage, it provides outstanding summer shade and brilliant fall color (unusual for a tree this adapted to tropical Gulf Coast conditions). The famous 'knees' — woody projections from roots that emerge above the waterline — appear primarily in flooded conditions and are thought to provide oxygen to waterlogged roots. Ancient specimens exceed 600 years old; the Montezuma Cypress (Taxodium mucronatum) of Mexico is related and evergreen. Bald Cypress is an ideal large specimen tree for rain gardens, pond edges, low-lying areas, and urban street plantings where other trees fail.
sources: USDA Forest Service silvics — Taxodium distichum
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Taxodium distichum
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Taxodium distichum

subcategory: native marginal aquatic tall bulrush reed

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in full sun in wet soil or shallow water (up to 12 inches deep) in zone 7–11; California Bulrush (Totora) is a tall, stout, round-stemmed native sedge found from the Pacific Coast through Central and South America — forming impressive stands of bright-green, round, rush-like stems reaching 6–10 feet that provide critical nesting, foraging, and cover habitat for wetland wildlife, and historically used by indigenous peoples for food, weaving, boat-building, and thatching.

directions: Plant Schoenoplectus californicus divisions in wet soil, pond margins, or in water up to 12 inches deep in full sun; plants spread by rhizomes to form large colonies — contain in aquatic pots for small ponds, or allow to naturalize in wildlife gardens; cut stems to the ground in late winter before new growth emerges. California Bulrush (formerly Scirpus californicus, also called Totora in South America) is one of the most important wetland plants of the Western Hemisphere — forming the dominant vegetation in freshwater marshes, pond margins, and estuaries from California and the Gulf Coast through Central and South America, including the famous floating reed islands of Lake Titicaca in Peru and Bolivia, where indigenous Uros people have built entire communities on platforms woven from Totora stems for centuries. The tall, cylindrical, bright-green stems are strongly ornamental in the water garden and provide exceptional value for wildlife: dense stands shelter nesting red-winged blackbirds, provide cover for waterfowl and marsh wrens, and support aquatic invertebrates vital to fish and amphibian populations. The seeds are eaten by ducks and other waterfowl. A fast-growing, vigorous species that establishes rapidly in disturbed wetlands and along pond banks, making it useful for erosion control and wetland restoration as well as ornamental use.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Center for Aquatic and Invasive Plants
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Schoenoplectus californicus
